w:document.mc:Ignorable: w14 w15 w16se w16cid w16 w16cex w16sdtdh w16sdtfl w16du wp14

w:r.w:t: requirement in the columns to the right. E.g. for Outstanding you must achieve Excellent plus the additional criteria
CO3201,3204 Final Mark Rubric – Undergraduate (FHEQ Level 6)
Depth and breadth of understanding beyond the scope of each aspect of the project.
Self-reflection demonstrates learning beyond expected undergraduate level.
Decisions or results are justified or explained showing deep understanding.
Knowledge is shown that is beyond what is directly required for the project.
Confident, accurate explanation of each aspect of the project.
Most aspects of the project are understood at a depth sufficient to explain them. Some are explained superficially or not clearly.
A superficial or unclear understanding of many aspects of the project and its outcome.
A little understanding is shown in most areas of the project.
it's not clear that the student understands their project.
Little evidence of understanding the project.
No evidence of knowledge relevant to the project.
Alternative methodologies were evaluated. One was chosen and adapted for this project.
Project evaluation shows a thorough understanding of how each aspect of the project could be improved.
The success of the approach to the project is evaluated and understood.
Systematic testing with the quality of the software clearly understood.
A software methodology was chosen with aspects of it successfully applied.
The approach is informed by the literature review.
Evaluation of whether requirements are met.
Awareness of software methodologies although perhaps not consistently applied.
All required sections of the dissertation are present.
Evidence of requirements, design and testing that contribute to any process successes. The role of these in are project are understood.
Evidence of at least 2 of requirements, design or testing that are relevant to the project.
An awareness of any weaknesses in the overall project.
An attempt to meet key requirements.
An awareness of where software quality issues (e.g. bugs) exist.
The project work is related to the plan or requirements but with no clear attempt to adhere to them.
Work has taken place in most
The choice of words and structure make this enjoyable and informative for both experts and undergraduates.
Text is easy and enjoyable to read.
Creative use of diagrams, tables or lists to significantly enhance the reader's understanding.
A comprehensive reflective account of the project.
Diagrams, tables or lists used appropriately to support the content.
Length of the write up is as expected.
All writing can be understood. Few grammar or language problems.
Use of tables, diagrams or lists.
Successful use of structure throughout. Most writing can be easily understood.
Structure has been used (e.g. sections, paragraphs).
Most of the writing can be understood with some effort.
Some elements of structure, but parts of the document require restructuring or rewriting to allow them to be understood.
Several sections cannot be understood or do not relate to the project.
Only a few sections can both be understood and relate to the project.
No meaningful content related to the project.
An approach to achieving maintainable code (e.g. SOLID) has been consistently applied.
Approaches applied and explained that ensure maintainability.
Code consistent with tiny or no
Can be understood, with little explanation.
Can be understand after explanation from student. Frequent minor coding
Student understands their code. Major issues but student understands this.
Student does not fully understand their code or its weaknesses.
Significant code exists but with many major
Student can explain some of it.
Evidence that considerations of future enhancements discussed in the Critical Appraisal have been incorporated into the design and architecture.
architecture evident in all aspects of implementation.
Evidence that architecture and design principles have been applied for most parts of the system, although not always successfully.
Improvements to the design would be required to allow this to be developed into a working system.
Basic structuring of code (e.g. classes, functions) has been applied to more than half of the system.
Basic structuring of code has been applied in a small number of cases.
No clear structure to the software.
A user study of the system with the intended users.
A simple, robust installation process has been created. E.g. an installer.
can work out how to use the system.
Could be used by some users but likely to cause frustration.
Guidance on host system requirements and/or installation
Potentially usable system, although users may require assistance.
Parts of the system could be used, although human assistance would be required for many users.
System can be used with direct, frequent support from the developer.
The system is usable by the developer only.
The developer struggles to use the system.
The developer cannot use their own system.
An innovative approach has been used to comprehensively solve a problem beyond expectations of an undergraduate.
that make this a final year project have been fully solved.
A working software system with multiple features but more
features of the project have not been completed.
problem has been solved by this project.
Progress has been made on solving problems or writing features.
Features or problems have been tackled but without significant success.
Mastery of both technologies and analytical skills to solve a
Application of advanced analytical, engineering, theoretical of mathematical techniques.
Application of deep familiarity with tools and technologies.
Competent use of technologies or tools.
Enough understanding of technology or tools to solve a problem in an appropriate way.
Technologies or tools applied with some success without a full understanding of why it works.
The student can explain why their approach has not been successful.
Insufficient skill is evident to successfully complete the project. Student can't explain why things don't work.
Student does not understand what skills they would need to complete the project.
Little evidence of applying technologies or tools to solve a problem.
Choice of content and the delivery led to a highly engaging and informative viva.
Project explained within its wider context. The key challenges understood by the listener.
Listener understands the aims and outcomes of the project, and the justification for key decisions.
Presentation of an appropriate length was given.
Project described but at a limited depth.
other areas not fully understood or explained.
General understanding provided but some key
understanding but only by for the listener to understand
Student did not appear to understand the project.
No meaningful or relevant information given.
Answers show deep knowledge beyond the scope of the project.
Constructive answers to questions within project scope.
Student aware of the limitations of their knowledge.
Questions answered directly with some clarifications required.
Directly answered half or more of the questions with useful and accurate information.
Unclear answers to more than half of the questions.
Responded to questions but often without a relevant or accurate answers.
All questions were responded to but unclearly.
Some relevant information in relation to questions.
Not able to respond meaningfully to questions.
Step marking is used for this submission, using the steps
shown in row 16. See the link below for details.
